hero.line1: Anxiety doesn't wait for your next session.
hero.subtitle: Talk to Mira when it's hard. She'll listen, help you untangle your thoughts, and won't say "just don't think about it."
hero.founderQuote: A year ago I was lying on the floor of an empty apartment in Ireland, unable to breathe. Divorce, panic attacks, antidepressants. Therapy helped — once a week. The other 6 days I'd open an AI chat just to get the thoughts out of my head. It didn't replace my therapist — but it filled the void between sessions. I built MindBridge so that void wouldn't be so unbearable for anyone.

stats.label1: of people with anxiety never get help
stats.label2: average wait for a first therapy appointment
stats.label3: minutes between sessions you're on your own

bridge.title: Not just a chatbot. A bridge to your therapist.
bridge.subtitle: Mira connects your daily experiences with your therapist's work — so you're both on the same page.
bridge.result: Result: you don't waste 20 minutes recapping your week. Instead — straight to the real work.

twoPaths.title: Works both ways
twoPaths.patientDesc: Start with Mira on your own. If you see a therapist — invite them. They'll see your progress, patterns and triggers. Sessions become more focused.
twoPaths.therapistDesc: Connect your patients to Mira. Get AI insights before each session: mood, topics, risks. Save 2+ hours per week on prep.

founder.p1: I've been anxious my whole life — I just didn't know there was a word for it. Sweaty palms behind the wheel, fear of phone calls, inability to tell people close to me what I feel. I thought everyone lived like that.
founder.p2: Then everything collapsed at once. Divorce after 10 years. Alone in Ireland — no friends, no family. Panic attacks, ambulances, cardiophobia. I was convinced I was dying — every morning, every night.
founder.p3: A doctor prescribed antidepressants. I found a therapist. It got better — but therapy is 50 minutes a week. The other 10,030 minutes I was alone with my fear.
founder.p4: One night I opened an AI chat. Not because I believed in it — but because there was literally nobody else to write to. And it asked the right question. Not 'rate your mood from 1 to 10' but 'what specifically scares you right now?' I let it all out. And it got a little easier.
founder.p5: That's when I understood: between sessions, people don't need a CBT textbook. They need someone who'll listen at 3 AM and won't say 'just don't think about it.' I built that someone.

howItWorks.title: How it works
howItWorks.step1Desc: Mira doesn't give textbook advice. She listens, asks questions, and helps you figure it out yourself.
howItWorks.step2Desc: "Always" and "never" are anxiety's favorite words. Mira helps you tell thoughts from facts.
howItWorks.step3Desc: Show up to therapy not with "it was an okay week" — but with real observations and data.

features.title: Everything you need between sessions
features.f1Desc: Structured cognitive behavioral therapy conversations, available any time of day or night.
features.f2Desc: Log how you feel and which emotions are present. See trends over days and weeks.
features.f3Desc: After every session: automatic analysis of emotions, thought patterns, and progress.
features.f4Desc: Your conversations stay between you and Mira. We never sell your data.

therapists.title: For therapists
therapists.subtitle: Understand your patients between sessions
therapists.body: Your patients talk to Mira between appointments. You get AI-generated insights before each session — so you can focus on what matters.
therapists.b1: See mood trends, anxiety and depression levels
therapists.b2: Get AI summaries — save 2+ hours per week
therapists.b3: Color-coded patient list: who needs attention now
therapists.b4: Generate detailed session reports in seconds

pricing.title: 7 days free. Decide after.
pricing.note: Save 33% with yearly billing. Message packs for extra flexibility.

demos.tagline: Not a generic helpful chatbot. Carefully crafted for real help.
demos.d1u1: i've been having tingling in my left chest. for two days now. i'm scared it's my heart. google says it could be a heart attack
demos.d1m1: Tingling is unsettling, I get why you're scared. Have you been checked out before? ECG, anything?
demos.d1u2: yeah like six months ago. ecg, echo, stress test. everything was normal
demos.d1m2:
Full workup six months ago — all clear. And now you're googling heart attack symptoms. Notice: you're trusting two minutes on Google over THREE medical tests. That's not logic — that's anxiety looking for confirmation.

Quick check: does the tingling change when you press on it? When you twist? Heart pain DOESN'T depend on position or pressure. Muscle pain does.
demos.d1u3: i mean yeah it hurts more when i press... but what if its still my heart?
demos.d1m3:
Stop. Let's look at what's happening in your head right now.

The event — tingling in your chest. A fact, neutral. Then your brain generates a thought: "it's a heart attack, I'm going to die." And from THAT THOUGHT — not the tingling — comes the fear, the panic, the 3am googling.

But here's the question: what's the evidence it's your heart? Not the feeling — actual evidence.
demos.d1m4:
And against? Three tests came back normal. Pain changes when you press — heart pain DOESN'T DO THAT. You're 30 and physically active.

See? One "evidence" — a sensation. Against it — three medical facts. But anxiety takes one sensation and turns it into a death sentence. That's called catastrophizing — and it's lying to you.

The problem isn't your heart. The problem is you can't tolerate uncertainty. "What if" isn't a question — it's an anxiety habit. And googling isn't checking, it's a compulsion that MAKES the fear worse.
demos.d2u1: had a fight with my girlfriend. i do so much for her — pay for everything, gifts, all of it. but when im feeling terrible she won't even come ask whats wrong. honestly it hurts
demos.d2m1: I hear the hurt. What exactly happened? You were feeling bad — and what did she do?
demos.d2u2: i was feeling awful, just lay there all evening. she sat on her phone the whole time. didn't even ask
demos.d2m2: Did you tell her you were feeling bad?
demos.d2u3: i mean its obvious. im lying there, silent, not doing anything. is she blind?
demos.d2m3:
No. But she's not a mind reader either. Let's look at what actually happened.

Event: you lay there in silence. That's a fact. Then your brain goes: "she SEES I'm suffering and she doesn't care." But that's not a fact — it's your interpretation. She might have seen you and thought "he's resting, tired day."

And from the thought "she doesn't care" — comes the hurt, the anger, the feeling you're alone. All of that from an untested thought.
demos.d2u4: but she really could have asked...
demos.d2m4:
Could have. Or could have genuinely not realized — and that doesn't mean she doesn't care. People can't read minds, even the ones who love you most.

But here's what I'm curious about: why DIDN'T you say something? What stopped you?
demos.d2u5: i don't know. feels weird to ask
demos.d2m5:
There it is. THAT'S the real problem. Not her — you. It's hard for you to say "I need help." So instead you stay quiet, wait, get disappointed, stockpile resentment. Then blow up or walk away. Sound familiar?

It's a pattern. And it keeps repeating not because you keep finding the "wrong" people — but because you keep staying silent.

One experiment: next time, say it directly. "I feel bad, come be with me." Not a hint, not silence — words. See what happens.
demos.d3u1: ive tried everything. breathing, meditation, exercise, journaling. nothing works. i feel like crap every day
demos.d3m1: Sounds like you're drained. How long has this been going on?
demos.d3u2: like two months. anxiety every morning. tried everything people recommend, no point
demos.d3m2: Let's figure out what "tried everything" means. Breathing — how often were you doing it? Every day on a schedule, or just when it was already bad?
demos.d3u3: when i was feeling bad obviously. why would i breathe when im fine
demos.d3m3:
Stop. Right here.

You're using techniques like an ambulance — only when it's already on fire. But they DON'T WORK that way. It's like taking blood pressure meds only when your head hurts.

Let's break it down: event — you feel bad in the morning. Thought — "nothing helps, I've tried everything, I'm hopeless." Result — despair, you give up, drop the technique.

But the thought "nothing helps" — is that true? Or did you just use the tools differently from how they're designed?
demos.d3u4: so its not that nothing works for me, its that im doing it wrong?
demos.d3m4:
You're NOT broken. And the techniques aren't useless. It's just that a fire truck doesn't replace a smoke detector — you need the second one and you've only been using the first.

Breathing, meditation — they train your nervous system. They work with consistency: every day, same time, REGARDLESS of how you feel. Two weeks minimum before anything shifts.

One thing. Not five — one. 4-7-8 breathing, ten minutes, every morning after your alarm. Two weeks, no skipping. Don't judge until end of week two. Deal?

cta.title: You don't have to handle this alone.
cta.subtitle: Free. No credit card. 50 messages on us.
cta.disclaimer: Mira is an AI companion trained in CBT techniques. She's here to support you between sessions — not to diagnose or treat. If you're in crisis, please call a crisis line or go to your nearest emergency room.

footer.copyright: © 2026 MindBridge. Not a replacement for professional help.

pricing.faqRunOutQ: What happens when I run out of messages?
pricing.faqRunOutA: You can buy a message pack to continue immediately, or wait until your plan resets next month. Your data and sessions are never deleted.
pricing.faqPacksExpireA: No. Purchased message packs stay in your account until used, regardless of your subscription status.
pricing.faqSecurityA: Yes. All conversations are encrypted. We never sell your data. Your therapist only sees what you choose to share.
